nativeRange.habitat: Lowland rainforest streams and coastal pools; also inhabits grass-plain streams with patchy rainforest cover. Found in the Kemp Welch and Collingwood river systems and coastal drainages between Port Moresby and Milne Bay.

decorPreferences: dense planting along sides and back (Java fern, Vallisneria, Hygrophila)
open central swimming lane for active mid-water shoaling
fine-leaved plants or spawning mops for egg deposition
subdued or dappled lighting to replicate rainforest stream canopy
dark substrate to intensify male coloration display

diet.notes: Accepts a wide variety of foods. Offer high-quality flake, spirulina flake, and micro-pellets as a staple. Supplement 2–3 times per week with small frozen or live foods: brine shrimp, daphnia, mosquito larvae, bloodworms, or tubifex. Wild fish feed on filamentous algae, insects, and invertebrates. Including vegetable-based and algae foods supports natural diet balance and colour.

avoidWith: very small fish that could be outcompeted at feeding time
slow-moving long-finned species (fin harassment risk from male sparring)

summary: Parkinson’s Rainbowfish is a robust, brilliantly coloured species endemic to coastal lowland streams of south-eastern Papua New Guinea. Display males develop a striking two-tone pattern: silver-blue on the front half and vivid golden-orange on the rear, with deep bodies and extended fins. An active mid-water shoaler suited to beginners.
careNotes: Parkinson’s Rainbowfish (Melanotaenia parkinsoni) is endemic to the coastal lowland streams and pools of south-eastern Papua New Guinea, ranging from the Port Moresby area east to Milne Bay, within the Kemp Welch and Collingwood river catchments. In the wild these streams run through lowland rainforest and grass plains and tend toward warm, slightly alkaline, moderately hard conditions. A tank of at least 120 cm in length (roughly 210 L for a typical 120×45×40 cm footprint) is advisable for a shoal of six or more; the species is an active mid-water swimmer and uses the full horizontal length of the tank. Maintain temperature between 26–30°C and pH 7.0–8.0 with moderate hardness; the species tolerates a wider pH range than many rainbowfish but does not fare well in soft acidic water. Good oxygenation is important — a power filter or surface agitation is recommended. Males are most colourful when kept with females and rival males, but sparring between males is generally harmless bluster. The male’s back half transitions from silver-blue to a deep reflective golden-orange at sexual maturity, making it one of the more visually striking members of the genus. The mouth is relatively narrow and the species will not predate on tankmates too large to be swallowed. Nitrite and ammonia must be kept at zero; do not add to an immature aquarium. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are recommended to keep nitrates below 15 ppm.
breedingNotes: Parkinson’s Rainbowfish are continual egg-scatterers that will spawn over fine-leaved plants or spawning mops over many days. Eggs are adhesive and hatch in approximately 8–10 days depending on temperature; warmer water shortens incubation. Parents and tankmates will eat eggs and fry; transfer spawning mops to a dedicated rearing vessel containing 75% mature tank water and 25% fresh dechlorinated water for best yields. Fry are tiny at hatching and require infusoria or commercial fry food initially, progressing to micro-worms and newly hatched brine shrimp within the first two weeks. Raise water temperature toward the upper end of the range (28–30°C) to encourage spawning. Sexing is straightforward in adults: males are larger, deeper-bodied, and display the characteristic orange rear coloration; females are smaller and more uniformly silver-olive.

primary.name: FishBase — Melanotaenia parkinsoni
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic reference. Confirmed family Melanotaeniidae, order Atheriniformes, adult size males 11.0 cm SL / females 9.0 cm SL, native range Papua New Guinea (Kemp Welsh River to Milne Bay), habitat (lowland rainforest streams), temperature 26–30°C, pH 7.5–7.8, hardness ≥8 dH, trophic level 2.9 (omnivore), IUCN Near Threatened (2019). Also cross-referenced via FishBase summary ID 25638.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping (Fishkeeper.co.uk) — Parkinson’s Rainbowfish
additional.notes: Practical Fishkeeping UK species profile. Provided adult size 15 cm, temperature 26–30°C, pH 6.5–8.0, hardness up to 18 dH, minimum 4 ft tank, diet (flake, green flake, micropellets, frozen mosquito larvae and daphnia), breeding as continual spawners with 8–10 day egg hatch, and note that high O2 levels are required.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity — Parkinson’s Rainbowfish
additional.notes: Care overview. Confirmed temperature 27–30°C, alkaline pH ~7.6–7.8, minimum 13 cm adult size, habitat in coastal lowland streams and pools from Port Moresby to Milne Bay. Natural diet includes filamentous algae and insects. Noted egg hatch in ~10 days at recommended temperatures; fry require infusoria then brine shrimp nauplii.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Parkinson’s Rainbowfish
additional.notes: Additional care data. Provided adult size 10–11 cm, temperature 21–25°C (notably lower than other sources), pH 7–8, hardness 8–18°N, native range (Collingwood and Kemp Welch river systems), lifespan 5–7 years, swim zone (middle levels). Diet: high-quality flakes/micropellets plus frozen/live foods. Egg scatterers spawning over fine-leaved plants, hatch in 7–10 days.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Melanotaenia parkinsoni
additional.notes: Supplementary reference. Confirmed common name Orange Rainbowfish, described by Allen 1980, endemic to western Papua New Guinea (Kemp Welsh River and Milne Bay), adult size generally to 15 cm, dark lavender base coloration with rosy chest, males with deeper bodies, extended fins, and golden-orange rear half. IUCN Near Threatened.

fish.breedingNotes: Continual egg-scatterers; spawn readily over fine-leaved plants or spawning mops at 28–30°C. Eggs hatch in 8–10 days. Remove spawning medium to a rearing vessel to prevent predation. Fry require infusoria initially, then micro-worms and brine shrimp nauplii.
